none: Polychlorinated biphenyls, Proposed rule making : environmental impact statement.

The FDA proposed new rules to limit human exposure to polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) in food and food packaging. These rules aim to prevent accidental contamination by banning certain PCB-containing materials in animal feed and food packaging. Temporary tolerances for unavoidable PCB residues in food and packaging are also established. The proposal is intended to reduce long-term exposure to PCBs, which are toxic and have been found in various foods due to industrial accidents and environmental contamination. While the rules may impact the recycling industry, they are seen as necessary for public health.
Polychlorinated biphenyls, Proposed rule making : environmental impact statement.

The FDA proposed new rules to limit human exposure to polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) in food and food packaging. These rules aim to prevent accidental contamination by banning certain PCB-containing materials in animal feed and food packaging. Temporary tolerances for unavoidable PCB residues in food and packaging are also established. The proposal is intended to reduce long-term exposure to PCBs, which are toxic and have been found in various foods due to industrial accidents and environmental contamination. While the rules may impact the recycling industry, they are seen as necessary for public health.

The proposed rule making regarding polychlorinated biphenyls (PCBs) was initiated due to growing concerns over the health risks associated with these toxic chemicals, which have been linked to various health issues and have contaminated food supplies. There was some public pushback, particularly from industries that would be affected by the new regulations, such as the recycling sector. Ultimately, the FDA moved forward with the proposal, emphasizing the importance of protecting public health from the dangers of PCB exposure.
United States. Food and Drug Administration
"The proposed rule making will considerably reduce accidental contamination and long-term exposure of man to PCB's in the United States." — Department of Agriculture

"Industry anticipates that a number of the recycling mills would close if the 5 ppm tolerance is implemented and that such closing would entail adverse environmental consequences." — Comments from the paper industry and related trade associations
